items.evidence: The official Library of Congress / Smithsonian NMAAHC transcript of the Marilyn Hildreth interview (AFC 2010/039, crhp0012, transcribed by Sally Council, August 13, 2011, page 8) renders this passage verbatim as: "And Mom said, told us, told Calvin and I, said, 'These people are following us...'" The Whisper rendering "Tokyo and then I said" is a phonetic mishearing of "told Calvin and I, said" — Clara Luper is naming her two children in the car (her son Calvin and her daughter Marilyn, the interviewee). There is no nickname "Tokyo" for Calvin in any archival source (Voices of Oklahoma interview, Oklahoma Historical Society encyclopedia entry, BlackPast.org, Bluegrass Special obituary, Clara Luper Legacy Committee bio, or the LoC Calvin Luper interview AFC 2010/039 crhp0013). The Pass 1 hypothesis ("and Marilyn") was a reasonable phonetic guess but is contradicted by the authoritative Smithsonian transcript.

items.original_candidate: Tokyo (and Marilyn) I said — speaker's brother nickname uncertain
items.evidence: Same passage as 82.19; Pass 2 retained the Pass 1 "Tokyo" hypothesis and speculated it was a family nickname for the brother Calvin. The official LoC/Smithsonian transcript (Sally Council, 2011, page 8) shows the actual phrasing is "told Calvin and I, said" — Clara Luper addressing her two children Calvin and Marilyn in the car as the pickup truck pursued them home from the freedom rally. No archival source documents any "Tokyo" nickname for Calvin Luper; he is referred to simply as Calvin or, in fuller name forms, Calvin Brady Luper. The Whisper engine misheard "told Calvin" as "Tokyo" and dropped the names of the addressees. This adjudication harmonizes with Marilyn Hildreth's own retelling in her Voices of Oklahoma interview, which describes the same pickup-truck-pursuit incident without any nickname being used.
